description: This neuron detects the beginning of a new line or paragraph, particularly after a title, heading, or comment delimiter (/**).
This neuron detects the first token or beginning portion of proper nouns, titles, and document markers, particularly at the start of sentences or documents.
This neuron detects proper nouns, particularly names of people and places, often at the beginning of titles, articles, or biographical entries.
The neuron detects hyphens that are part of HTML/XML attribute syntax in academic citation references, specifically in the "ref-type" attribute.
This neuron detects line breaks or paragraph boundaries, particularly those that occur after titles, headers, or introductory text and before the start of new content or body text.
This neuron detects the first two or three characters of proper nouns, technical terms, or standardized prefixes in formal or academic contexts.
This neuron detects line breaks or newlines that occur immediately after metadata, headers, timestamps, or version identifiers at the beginning of documents or messages.
This neuron detects the first letter "H" at the beginning of proper nouns, names, and titles.

This neuron detects truncated or abbreviated word tokens at the beginning of proper nouns, technical terms, or sentence-initial words, often appearing in titles, headings, or the start of quoted text.
This neuron detects period characters that separate package name components in Java/Kotlin package declarations and similar hierarchical namespace identifiers.

This neuron detects technical terminology related to enclosed spaces or systems where processes occur, particularly in engineering contexts (combustion chambers, cache memory) and structural/organizational components (body sections, configuration elements).
This neuron detects the start of a new line or section in formal legal, technical, or official documents, particularly after document identifiers, case numbers, or section headers.

This neuron detects pronouns or words that refer back to previously mentioned subjects or topics in the text, often serving as anaphoric references or the start of clauses that continue a previously established idea.
A newline token immediately following the start of a multi-line comment block (/*) in source code or copyright notices.
This neuron appears to detect leading whitespace or indentation at the beginning of legal document headers, particularly before court names, case designations, and official filing information.

This neuron detects formatting elements in citation references and parenthetical numerical expressions, particularly focusing on opening parentheses and numbers that appear in academic reference formatting or statistical data.
Semicolons that terminate variable declarations or statements immediately before a for-loop initialization.
This neuron detects the opening delimiter `<?` that begins processing instructions or code blocks in markup and scripting languages (XML declarations and PHP tags).
This neuron detects the byte order mark (BOM) character at the beginning of files, which is an invisible Unicode character (U+FEFF) commonly used to indicate text encoding.
This neuron detects word fragments or suffixes, particularly incomplete words that are part of technical, medical, or specialized terminology.
The neuron appears to detect the prefix "cro" at the beginning of words, particularly before words like "crocodile," "crochet," "crop," and "cropped."
This neuron detects legal terminology, specifically words related to liability and compensation in software license disclaimers (such as "DAMAGES" and "CLAIM").

This neuron detects the prefix "mis-" when it appears at the beginning of words that indicate error, wrongdoing, or negative deviation from correctness (such as misconstrued, misinterpreting, misguided, misregulation, misplace, misperceptions, and misinterpret).
This neuron detects the word "civil" when it appears in formal or institutional contexts, particularly in phrases like "civil rights," "civil war," "civil servant," and "civil authorities."
This neuron detects patent numbers in U.S. Patent citations, specifically firing on the last three digits of the patent number.
This neuron detects the string "equiv" in HTML meta tag attributes (particularly "http-equiv") and in scientific notation for chemical equivalents.

This neuron detects the word "KIND" in software license boilerplate text, specifically in the phrase "WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND" which appears in Apache and similar open-source licenses.
This neuron detects the prefix "hom-" at the beginning of scientific, medical, or technical terms (such as homogenous, homozygote, homocysteine, homoplastic, homonym, homage).
This neuron detects uppercase letter sequences that are part of abbreviated or truncated words, particularly in technical documentation, legal text, and code comments.

This neuron detects the prefix "inter-" when it appears as part of compound words or technical terms indicating "between" or "mutual" relationships (e.g., intermolecular, interneurons, interference, interlocking).

This neuron detects the beginning of proper nouns and brand names that start with "Gar", including personal names (Garry), place names (Garithos), company names (Garmin), and other entities (Garagum, Garay).
The neuron appears to detect the prefix "bi-" at the start of scientific, medical, and technical compound words.
This neuron detects the token "cur" when it appears as a prefix or beginning fragment of various words like "curation," "courtesy," "curfew," "curvy," "curatively," and "curcumin."
This neuron detects the word "Nobel" or "Peace" when appearing in contexts related to the Nobel Peace Prize, particularly in discussions about Malala Yousafzai's nomination and award.
This neuron detects the word "OF" when it appears in formal legal document headers between the words "COURT" and "APPEALS" in titles of judicial courts.
This neuron detects the word "arthroplasty" or its abbreviated form "arthro" in medical contexts, specifically relating to joint replacement surgical procedures.
This neuron detects the beginning of Apache mod_rewrite directives, specifically the "Rewrite" prefix that starts RewriteCond and RewriteRule statements in .htaccess or Apache configuration files.
This neuron detects the prefix "poly" (meaning "many" or "multiple") in medical, scientific, and technical compound words.

This neuron detects the prefix "immun-" or "immune-" in medical and scientific contexts, particularly when it appears as part of compound words related to immunology, immune system functions, or immunological techniques.
This neuron detects the prefix "multi" at the beginning of compound words (like "multiselect," "multithreading," "multilingual," "multisite," "multibillions," "multivectors," "multiset").
This neuron detects the substring "whit" appearing at the beginning of words, particularly in proper nouns like "Whitman," technical terms like "whitelist," or words related to color like "white."

Looking at the pattern across these examples, the neuron appears to detect **a separator line consisting of repeated 'l' characters that appears after a party's name/role designation in a legal document heading**.
This neuron detects the term "autosomal" in the context of genetic inheritance patterns, particularly when describing hereditary conditions, chromosomes, or loci in medical and genetics literature.
This neuron detects uppercase tokens that are part of standard legal disclaimer or license text, particularly within software license boilerplate (such as BSD or Apache licenses).
This neuron detects hyphens that connect "all" with a following word to form compound adjectives or nouns (like "all-clear," "all-inclusive," "all-new," "all-time," "all-important," "all-pervasive").
